en.extract:
The Kangean shama (Copsychus nigricauda) is a medium sized passerine bird in the Old World flycatcher family Muscicapidae that is endemic to the Kangean Islands in Indonesia. It was formerly considered to be conspecific with the white-rumped shama. The species may be extinct in the wild.


Taxonomy
The Kangean shama was formally described in 1893 by the Dutch scientist Adolphe Vorderman under the binomial name Cittocincla nigricauda. The specific epithet combines the Latin niger meaning "black" with cauda meaning "tail". The Kangean shama is now placed with 16 other species in the genus Copsychus  that was introduced in 1827 by the German naturalist Johann Georg Wagler. It was formerly considered as subspecies of the white-rumped shama (Copsychus malabaricus) but is now treated as a separate species based on morphological differences and a molecular genetic study of museum specimens published in 2022. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
The Kangean shama has glossy blue-black upperparts, bright chestnut underparts apart from a white vent, a white rump and an almost entirely black tail with white tips to the outer feathers. The sexes are similar in plumage but the tail is shorter in females.


Conservation status
The Kangean shama is highly threatened by the pet trade and is possibly extinct in the wild.

The '''Kangean shama''' ('''''Copsychus nigricauda''''') is a medium sized [[passerine]] bird in the Old World flycatcher family [[Muscicapidae]] that is endemic to the [[Kangean Islands]] in Indonesia. It was formerly considered to be [[conspecific]] with the [[white-rumped shama]]. The species may be extinct in the wild.

==Taxonomy==
The Kangean shama was [[species description|formally described]] in 1893 by the Dutch scientist [[Adolphe Vorderman]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Cittocincla nigricauda''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Vorderman | first=Adolphe | author-link=Adolphe Vorderman | date=1893 | title=Bijdrage tot de kennis der vogels van den Kangean-archipel | language=Dutch | journal=Natuurkundig tijdschrift voor Nederlandsch Indië | volume=52 | pages=181-208 [197-198] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/39558829 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Paynter | editor2-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1964 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=10 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology  | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=72 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14486261 }}</ref> The specific epithet combines the [[Latin]] ''niger'' meaning "black" with ''cauda'' meaning "tail".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=nigricauda | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=nigricauda | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=14 November 2024 }}</ref> The Kangean shama is now placed with 16 other species in the genus ''[[Copsychus ]]'' that was introduced in 1827 by the German naturalist [[Johann Georg Wagler]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Chats, Old World flycatchers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/chats/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=14 November 2024}}</ref> It was formerly considered as [[subspecies]] of the [[white-rumped shama]] (''Copsychus malabaricus'') but is now treated as a separate species based on morphological differences and a molecular genetic study of museum specimens published in 2022.<ref name=ioc/><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Wu | first1=M.Y. | last2=Lau | first2=C.J. | last3=Ng | first3=E.Y.X. | last4=Baveja | first4=P. | last5=Gwee | first5=C.Y. | last6=Sadanandan | first6=K. | last7=Ferasyi | first7=T.R. | last8=Haminuddin | last9=Ramadhan | first9=R. | last10=Menner | first10=J.K. | last11=Rheindt | first11=F.E. | date=2022 | title=Genomes from historic DNA unveil massive hidden extinction and terminal endangerment in a tropical Asian songbird radiation | journal=Molecular Biology and Evolution | volume=39 | issue=9 | article-number=msac189 | doi=10.1093/molbev/msac189 | doi-access=free | pmc=9486911 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>
 
==Description==
The Kangean shama has glossy blue-black upperparts, bright chestnut underparts apart from a white vent, a white rump and an almost entirely black tail with white tips to the outer feathers. The sexes are similar in plumage but the tail is shorter in females.<ref name=bow>{{cite web | last1=Berryman | first1=A.J. | last2=Collar | first2=N. | last3=Kirwan | first3=G.M. | date=2024 | title=Kangean Shama (Copsychus nigricauda), version 1.0 | editor1-last=Keeney | editor1-first=B.K. | editor2-last=Billerman | editor2-first=S.M. | work=Birds of the World | location=Ithaca, NY, USA | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | url=https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.whrsha11.01 | access-date=14 November 2024 | url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Conservation status==
The Kangean shama is highly threatened by the pet trade and is possibly extinct in the wild.<ref name=bow/><ref>{{Cite journal | last=Berryman | first=Alex J. | date=2023 | title=Another Indonesian songbird on the brink of extinction: is it too late for the Kangean shama? | journal=Oryx | volume=57 | issue=5 | pages=556-557 | doi=10.1017/S0030605323000996 | doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Eaton | first1=J. | last2=Shepherd | first2=C. | last3=Rheindt | first3=F. | last4=Harris | first4=J.B.C. | last5=van Balen | first5=B. | last6=Wilcove | first6=D. | last7=Collar | first7=N. | date=2015 | title=Trade-driven extinctions and near-extinctions of avian taxa in Sundaic Indonesia | journal=Forktail | volume=31 | pages=1-12 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/58103762}}</ref>
